findings.business_consequence: EUR 557,000 of committed spend (91.9% of total) was released without the mandated second approval, exposing the company to fraud, overpayment, and unauthorized commitment risk on its largest orders.
findings.description:
Policy clause 2 requires both a Category Manager AND a second Finance approval for any purchase order above EUR 50,000. The export shows 7 of 12 POs (58.3%) carry approval_status 'single_approval_only', accounting for EUR 557,000 (91.9% of all PO value). The 5 fully 'approved' POs together sum to only EUR 49,100 — none exceeds the EUR 50,000 threshold — while the highest single PO is EUR 120,000. In effect every order requiring a second approval bypassed it. This systematic control failure is invisible in any single document.

What the data shows:
- POs with single_approval_only: 7
- Pct of rows single_approval_only: 58.3
- EUR value single_approval_only: 557,000
- Pct of value single_approval_only: 91.9
- EUR value of all fully-approved POs: 49,100
- Max single PO amount: 120,000
- Dual approval threshold rule: 50,000 (“2. Any purchase order above EUR 50,000 requires approval from the Category Manager AND a second” — Procurement Policy)
- Second approver: Finance (“approval from Finance before it is released to the supplier.” — Procurement Policy)

sources.quote: 2. Any purchase order above EUR 50,000 requires approval from the Category Manager AND a second

findings.title: Every PO above the EUR 50,000 dual-approval threshold received only single approval

findings.business_consequence: EUR 216,000 was committed to suppliers before any purchase order existed, defeating budget controls and three-way matching, and concentrating risk in orders that also lacked required approval.
findings.description:
Policy clause 4 states maverick spend — ordering without a purchase order — is 'not permitted under any circumstances'. Yet po_before_order = 'no' on 3 of 12 POs (25%), totalling EUR 216,000 (35.6% of total value). All 3 maverick orders also carry single_approval_only status (3 POs, EUR 216,000), violating both the PO-first rule and the dual-approval rule simultaneously. An absolute prohibition is breached on a quarter of transactions.

What the data shows:
- POs with po_before_order=no: 3
- Pct of rows po_before_order=no: 25
- EUR value of maverick POs: 216,000
- Maverick POs also single_approval_only (value): 216,000
- Maverick POs also single_approval_only (count): 3
- Maverick spend prohibition: not permitted (“4. Maverick spend — ordering without a purchase order — is not permitted under any circumstances.” — Procurement Policy)

sources.quote: 4. Maverick spend — ordering without a purchase order — is not permitted under any circumstances.

findings.title: Maverick spend banned by policy occurs on 25% of POs, worth EUR 216,000

findings.business_consequence: Repeat non-compliance is concentrated in one buyer (EUR 149,000), yet no document assigns anyone to own, monitor, or grant exceptions to the control, so violations go undetected and unremediated.
findings.description:
The maverick spend is not random: buyer_a alone accounts for 2 of the 3 no-PO orders worth EUR 149,000 (24.6% of total value), with buyer_d responsible for the remaining 1 (EUR 67,000). The policy explicitly frames itself as 'the controlled, approved procurement process' but contains no ownership, escalation, monitoring, or exception language: find_mentions returned zero hits for 'RACI', 'owner', 'exception', and 'monitor'. With no assigned accountability, the buyer-level breaches in F1/F2 have no remediation path and are likely to recur.

What the data shows:
- buyer_a maverick POs: 2
- buyer_a maverick PO value: 149,000
- buyer_a maverick pct of value: 24.6
- buyer_d maverick PO value: 67,000
- Policy self-description as the control process: controlled, approved procurement process (“4. Maverick spend — ordering without a purchase order — is not permitted under any circumstances.” — Procurement Policy)

current_state.domain_overview: This assessment covers how the company commits spend with suppliers through its purchase order process, from the point a buyer raises an order to the point that order is approved and committed. The review draws on the company's procurement policy and a recent export of purchase order records.

handoff_catalogue.mechanism: Order submitted by buyer for approval
Approved order released for supplier commitment

ownership_map.activity: Monitor control adherence and grant exceptions

process_flow.description: A buyer raises a purchase order against a supplier requirement and records the committed amount. In the records reviewed, the largest single order was EUR 120,000.
process_flow.name: Raise purchase order

process_flow.description: Policy requires a purchase order to exist before any commitment is made to a supplier. In the records reviewed, 3 orders worth EUR 216,000 were committed before a purchase order existed.
process_flow.name: Confirm order precedes supplier commitment

process_flow.description: The procurement policy requires a second approval for orders above EUR 50,000. In the records reviewed, 7 orders received only a single approval, representing EUR 557,000 of committed spend.
process_flow.name: Apply approval threshold

process_flow.description: Once approved, orders are committed to suppliers. No document assigns anyone to own, monitor, or grant exceptions to the sequencing and approval controls. The fully-approved orders in the records reviewed totalled EUR 49,100.
process_flow.name: Commit and monitor spend

process_inventory.name: Purchase order raising
process_inventory.purpose: Capture supplier commitments as formal orders before spend is committed.

process_inventory.name: Order approval
process_inventory.purpose: Apply the policy approval threshold, including a second approver above EUR 50,000.

process_inventory.name: Spend commitment and oversight
process_inventory.purpose: Commit approved orders to suppliers and monitor adherence to policy controls.

current_state.process_summary: Buyers raise purchase orders against supplier requirements and submit them for approval. The procurement policy sets the rules for when an order may be committed, including an approval threshold above which a second approver is required, and a sequencing rule requiring a purchase order to exist before any commitment is made to a supplier. In the records reviewed, 7 of the purchase orders received only a single approval, representing 58.3% of orders and EUR 557,000 of committed spend. A further 3 orders, representing 25.0% of orders and EUR 216,000 of value, were committed to suppliers before a purchase order existed.

system_inventory.name: Procurement policy
system_inventory.purpose: Defines the approval threshold, the second-approver requirement, and the order-before-commitment rule.

system_inventory.name: Purchase order records
system_inventory.purpose: Holds the record of raised orders, their committed amounts, and their approvals.

synthesis.dependency_notes: OPP2 depends on OPP1 because the order-first control is most effective once second approval is enforced on the same high-value orders. OPP3 depends on both OPP1 and OPP2 because monitoring is meaningful only once the controls it oversees are enforced.

after_process.description: Buyer raises an order and the committed amount is checked against the EUR 50,000 threshold.
after_process.name: Raise purchase order

after_process.description: First approver reviews and approves the order.
after_process.name: First approval

after_process.description: Orders above the threshold are routed to a second approver and cannot be committed until that approval is given.
after_process.name: Mandatory second approval above threshold

before_process.description: Buyer raises an order; the largest single order seen was EUR 120,000.
before_process.name: Raise purchase order

before_process.description: Order is committed after a single approval, even above the threshold. 7 orders worth EUR 557,000 followed this path.
before_process.name: Single approval and commit

business_impact.narrative: Enforcing the second approval at the point of commitment brings the company's largest orders back under control. It directly addresses the EUR 557,000 of spend, across 7 orders and 91.9% of total value, that was committed on a single approval.

opportunities.implementation_approach: Embed the approval threshold and second-approver routing into the order approval step, with a hard stop preventing commitment until the second sign-off is recorded.
opportunities.overview: Build the policy's second-approval requirement directly into the order approval step so that no order above EUR 50,000 can be committed until a second approver has signed off.
risks: Approver availability could slow commitments if no delegation is in place
success_metrics: No orders above the threshold committed on a single approval
All orders above EUR 50,000 carry a recorded second approval
opportunities.title: Enforced second approval above the threshold

after_process.description: A purchase order is raised and validated before any supplier commitment can proceed.
after_process.name: Raise and validate order

after_process.description: Supplier commitment is blocked unless a valid order already exists, removing the EUR 216,000 order-after-commit path.
after_process.name: Commit only against an existing order

before_process.description: Buyer commits to a supplier before an order exists. 3 orders worth EUR 216,000, or 25.0% of orders, followed this path, all on a single approval.
before_process.name: Commit to supplier

before_process.description: An order is recorded after the commitment has already been made.
before_process.name: Document order afterwards

business_impact.narrative: Requiring an order before commitment restores budget control and three-way matching. It addresses the EUR 216,000, across 3 orders and 25.0% of orders, committed before any order existed. Because all of this value also carries the single-approval issue, the EUR 216,000 also benefits from the enforced second approval.

quantified.label: Value committed without a prior order
Value also affected by single approval

opportunities.implementation_approach: Add an order-exists validation as a precondition to supplier commitment, building on the enforced approval step so commitments cannot proceed without both a valid order and the required approvals.
opportunities.overview: Require that a valid purchase order exists before any commitment can be made to a supplier, closing the path that lets spend happen before an order is raised.
risks: Urgent purchases may need a controlled fast-track exception path
success_metrics: No supplier commitments made before a valid order exists
Three-way matching possible on all orders
opportunities.title: Order-first control before supplier commitment

after_process.description: A named owner is accountable for the approval and order-first controls and for granting exceptions.
after_process.name: Assign control owner

after_process.description: Order activity is monitored continuously, surfacing concentrations such as the EUR 149,000 in one buyer for timely review.
after_process.name: Continuous monitoring and alerting

after_process.description: Flagged cases are reviewed, remediated, and any exceptions documented.
after_process.name: Remediate and document exceptions

before_process.description: No one is assigned to monitor adherence, so concentrated non-compliance of 2 orders worth EUR 149,000, or 24.6% of value, goes undetected.
before_process.name: No monitoring

before_process.description: Repeat behaviour persists with no exception or remediation process.
before_process.name: No remediation

business_impact.narrative: A named owner with continuous monitoring catches concentrated non-compliance before it persists. It targets the 2 orders worth EUR 149,000, or 24.6% of value, concentrated in one buyer, and provides the oversight layer that makes the enforced controls durable.

opportunities.implementation_approach: Define accountability for the controls in the policy, then stand up continuous monitoring that watches order activity against the enforced approval and order-first rules and alerts the owner to concentrations and exceptions.
opportunities.overview: Assign an accountable owner for the approval and order-first controls, supported by ongoing monitoring that surfaces concentrated non-compliance such as the pattern seen in one buyer.
risks: Monitoring is only as effective as the enforced controls it watches
success_metrics: Every control has a named accountable owner
Concentrated non-compliance surfaced and remediated within a defined window
opportunities.title: Named owner and monitoring for procurement controls

pain_points.description: Every purchase order above the EUR 50,000 dual-approval threshold received only a single approval. This accounts for 7 orders representing EUR 557,000 of committed spend, which is 91.9% of the total value reviewed.
pain_points.failure_pattern: Control defined in policy but not enforced in the working process.

quantified.label: Orders with single approval only
quantified.text: 7 orders received only a single approval

pain_points.root_cause: The approval threshold exists in the policy but is not enforced at the point an order is committed, so a second approval can be skipped without anything stopping the commitment.
pain_points.title: Largest orders committed without the required second approval

pain_points.description: Spend banned by policy occurs on 25.0% of orders, worth EUR 216,000, where commitments were made to suppliers before a purchase order existed. All 3 of these orders also received only a single approval, with the full EUR 216,000 affected by both issues at once.
pain_points.failure_pattern: Sequencing control bypassed, compounding with the missing second approval on the same orders.

quantified.label: Orders committed before a purchase order existed
quantified.text: 3 orders committed before a purchase order existed

quantified.label: Value committed without a prior order
quantified.text: EUR 216,000 committed without a prior order

pain_points.root_cause: The policy rule requiring an order before any supplier commitment is not built into the working process, so buyers can commit first and document later.
pain_points.title: Spend committed to suppliers before any purchase order exists

pain_points.description: Repeat non-compliance is concentrated in a single buyer, accounting for 2 orders worth EUR 149,000, which is 24.6% of value. No document assigns anyone to own, monitor, or grant exceptions to the control, so the pattern goes undetected and unremediated.
pain_points.failure_pattern: Unowned control with no monitoring, allowing repeat behaviour to persist.
pain_points.root_cause: No accountable owner is assigned for monitoring control adherence, so concentrated non-compliance is never surfaced or addressed.
pain_points.title: Repeat non-compliance concentrated in one buyer, with no owner

items.rationale: Protects the EUR 557,000 of single-approval spend, 91.9% of total value, and is the prerequisite for the later controls.
items.title: Enforced second approval above the threshold

roadmap.theme: Enforce approval on the largest orders

items.rationale: Builds on enforced approval to remove the EUR 216,000 committed before an order existed, restoring budget control and matching.
items.title: Order-first control before supplier commitment

items.rationale: Adds durable oversight over the enforced controls, surfacing concentrated non-compliance such as the EUR 149,000 in one buyer.
items.title: Named owner and monitoring for procurement controls

synthesis.sequencing_rationale: The enforced second approval is the foundation: it directly protects the largest share of value (EUR 557,000, 91.9% of total) and nothing else depends on it being delayed, so it goes first. The order-first control builds on that enforcement, because the EUR 216,000 committed before an order existed is the same value that also lacked a second approval; closing the sequencing path on top of enforced approval gives the strongest combined effect, so it follows. The named owner and monitoring layer comes last because it watches over both enforced controls and is only as effective as the rules it monitors.
synthesis.strategic_readiness: The controls already exist in policy; the work is to enforce them in the working process and assign accountability. This is a high-readiness programme because the rules and the evidence are settled, and the first move is both the highest value and the most feasible.

strategy_profile.notes: Move from rules that exist on paper to controls enforced in the working process, sequenced from highest value and feasibility to durable oversight.
strategy_profile.posture: Control-first procurement: enforce the policy at the point of commitment, then own and monitor it.
